aliases: Burrata with Parma Ham and Rocket

english: Burrata with Parma Ham and Rocket

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for burrata-and-prosciutto as a composite dish; FOODON_03315671 (cheese-based dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ (Cheese dishes) is the closest available category for this assembled Italian appetizer combining burrata, cured ham, rocket, cherry tomatoes, and olives.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Italian appetizer dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard appetizer plate of approximately 250 g, comprising a ball of burrata cheese (~100 g), two to three slices of prosciutto di Parma (~50 g), a handful of rocket (~30 g), cherry tomatoes (~40 g), green olives (~20 g), and a drizzle of olive oil (~10 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy and Foundation Foods: Burrata cheese, Prosciutto (cured ham), Arugula, Cherry tomatoes, Green olives, Olive oil
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for this composite dish. Per-100g values are estimated from a weighted-ingredient blend using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for burrata-style fresh mozzarella (FDC 173420), prosciutto/cured ham (FDC 174608), raw arugula (FDC 169387), raw cherry tomatoes (FDC 170457), raw green olives (FDC 169094), and olive oil (FDC 171413). Approximate ingredient proportions: burrata 40%, prosciutto 20%, rocket 12%, cherry tomatoes 16%, olives 8%, olive oil 4%. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this blend and found broadly consistent; input values are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed cheese-and-cured-meat appetizer
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for burrata-and-prosciutto as a composite dish. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries: fresh mozzarella-type cheese (GI ~27), cured ham (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate), arugula and cherry tomatoes (GI ~15–38 for non-starchy vegetables), and green olives (GI ~15). The dish is predominantly fat and protein with only 6.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g, yielding a low GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (32 × 16.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 5.4, rounded to 5.6 accounting for olive oil contribution. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 600 mg per 100 g — primarily from prosciutto di Parma (a dry-cured meat) and burrata cheese; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1500 mg sodium, exceeding 65% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium content from cured prosciutto and cheese; if consumed, restrict to a small portion (≤80 g) and avoid adding any additional salt or salty condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (6.7 g/100 g) and low GI (~32) are favourable for glycaemic control, but high saturated fat (6.7 g/100 g) and caloric density (217 kcal/100 g) require portion discipline.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycaemic index and modest carbohydrate load, making it broadly acceptable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (≤150 g), but the high saturated fat from burrata and the elevated sodium from prosciutto warrant caution — pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid refined bread accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.7 g/100 g (exceeds the 5 g/100 g CVD threshold) combined with sodium 600 mg/100 g and cholesterol 40 mg/100 g from burrata and prosciutto; a 250 g serving provides 16.75 g saturated fat, approaching the daily limit for CVD patients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional indulgence only; the combination of high saturated fat from burrata cheese and high sodium from prosciutto di Parma poses dual risk for LDL elevation and blood pressure, and portion size should not exceed 100 g per occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat exceeds 5 g/100 g threshold; prosciutto is a processed cured meat contributing pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid; cherry tomatoes are nightshades which may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dish due to its processed cured meat content and saturated fat load; the anti-inflammatory benefit of olive oil and rocket does not offset the pro-inflammatory contribution of prosciutto and nightshade tomatoes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burrata and Prosciutto di Parma is a classic Italian cold appetizer (antipasto) originating from the culinary traditions of southern and northern Italy — burrata from Puglia and prosciutto from Emilia-Romagna. The dish is assembled rather than cooked: a whole ball of fresh burrata cheese, made from mozzarella curd filled with stracciatella (cream and mozzarella shreds), is placed at the centre of a plate and surrounded by paper-thin slices of dry-cured Parma ham, fresh rocket (arugula/rucola), sweet cherry tomatoes, and briny green olives, then finished with a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil and Italian seasoning. The flavour profile is a deliberate contrast of rich, milky, and buttery burrata against the salty, umami-forward prosciutto, the peppery bite of rocket, the acidity of tomatoes, and the brininess of olives. Nutritionally, the dish is high in fat (16 g/100 g) and protein (11.7 g/100 g) with moderate calories (217 kcal/100 g) and low carbohydrates (6.7 g/100 g), giving it a low glycaemic index of approximately 32. Sodium is high at 600 mg/100 g due to the cured ham and cheese, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Saturated fat at 6.7 g/100 g exceeds clinical thresholds for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis risk. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets and contains dairy allergens. It is served as a starter or light lunch in Italian restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it appears on Italian restaurant menus as a premium appetizer.

confidence_reason: Input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 and provenance from a food delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary source. Per-100g values have been cross-validated against a weighted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient blend and found broadly consistent. Medium confidence is assigned because no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish; values are estimated from component ingredients.
notes: potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite assembled dish (burrata + prosciutto + rocket + cherry tomatoes + olives) are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual ingredient entries exist but a validated composite blend for all micronutrients was not computed to avoid compounding estimation error. monounsaturated_fat_g (5.8 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g) are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles of burrata-style mozzarella and olive oil components. The input record incorrectly tagged this dish as gluten_free in dietary_tags; prosciutto di Parma may be processed in facilities handling gluten and the dish is not certified gluten-free — this tag has been removed. The input record listed two portions both labelled '1 appetizer plate' with different weights (250 g and 300 g) and both marked is_default=true; 250 g has been selected as the default serving size as the more conservative and commonly cited restaurant portion. The input record's country_of_origin was listed as Singapore, which reflects the restaurant location rather than the dish's culinary origin; region_of_origin is correctly set to Italy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component data; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in peer-reviewed literature.
